lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. contractors providing repeat coal-mine development, drilling, blasting, excavation, dewatering, equipment operation, maintenance, ventilation, reclamation support, and other on-site technical or operating services to external mine owners.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive mine-owner crews; coal extraction for the firm's own account; equipment-only dealers; commodity traders; one-off consultants without recurring field delivery; shells; and businesses dominated by oil, gas, or noncoal mining support.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat project, unit-rate, time-and-materials, and contracted field services sold to coal mine operators, limited to transferable external-customer firms plausibly generating $1-10 million of normalized EBITDA.

a.bridge: BLS coal-mining employment, used as the closest task proxy for coal-support crews, shows 55.43% of workers in construction and extraction occupations, including 12.05% construction equipment operators; broader mining support has 34.37% extraction workers and 5.77% installation/maintenance/repair workers. I translate this predominantly physical, regulated field mix into a minority wage-weighted AI-exposed share concentrated in dispatch, estimating, safety records, maintenance planning, reporting, and technical analysis. The mine-operator proxy may understate contractor administration but overstate exposure where contractors specialize in manual field work.
a.rationale: AI can reduce information-processing and coordination labor, while drilling, blasting, equipment operation, repair, hauling, inspection, and accountable safety work remain embodied.
a.change_evidence: Six-digit payroll and time-on-task studies for coal-support contractors, distinguishing field, shop, engineering, safety, dispatch, and administrative labor.
caveats: Neither source directly reports the six-digit contractor task mix.
Occupation shares are not wage-weighted AI exposure and include already automated work.

rho.rationale: Dispatch, bid preparation, compliance documentation, maintenance analytics, and reporting can adopt practical tools within five years, but connectivity, data quality, safety validation, union/work rules, and small-firm change capacity constrain implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Measured deployments at coal contractors showing workflow coverage, error rates, review burden, adoption costs, and labor hours avoided.
caveats: Remote or highly standardized surface operations may implement faster than underground and specialty crews.

e.rationale: Many contractors fit repeat outsourced external service, but the small frozen universe may include captive affiliates, diversified mining-support firms, equipment lessors, subscale operators, or businesses outside the EBITDA band.
e.change_evidence: Firm-by-firm ownership, NAICS revenue mix, customer contracts, normalized EBITDA, and operating-status verification for the frozen 17-firm population.
caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged from an oilfield-services margin and is especially uncertain for coal contractors.

s5.rationale: Owner succession and industry consolidation can produce sales, but mine closures, bonding, equipment condition, customer concentration, and uncertain coal demand can make firms unattractive or lead to liquidation instead of qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed control transactions, owner-age and succession data, and sale-versus-liquidation outcomes for U.S. coal-support contractors.
caveats: No observed six-digit control-transfer rate was found.

q.rationale: Unit-rate and time-and-material contracts can preserve gains until rebid, and safety performance or scarce equipment supports differentiation; a concentrated, declining customer base can demand lower rates and capture productivity at renewal.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, escalation and gain-sharing clauses, bid histories, renewal repricing, customer concentration, and realized margins after productivity initiatives.
caveats: Emergency and specialized work may retain more than competitively bid routine services.

d5.bridge: EIA's April 2026 short-term outlook projects U.S. coal production falling from 533 million short tons in 2025 to 517 in 2026 and 494 in 2027, while AEO2026 says coal-plant retirements drive demand lower and policy could cause coal generation to mostly disappear. I extend declining coal output and retirement pressure into a five-year support-service quantity range, allowing partial resilience because reclamation, closure, maintenance, and contractor outsourcing can rise even as production falls. Production may overstate decline for reclamation specialists and understate it for extraction-tied contractors.
d5.rationale: Coal-support volume is tightly linked to mine production and capital activity, both structurally pressured, although exports, metallurgical coal, required reclamation, and outsourcing preserve a meaningful service base.
d5.change_evidence: Mine-by-mine five-year production, closure, development, export, and reclamation plans mapped to contractor revenue by basin and customer.
caveats: Coal outcomes are highly policy-, weather-, export-, and natural-gas-price-sensitive.
The service basket spans production-linked and closure-linked activities with opposite trends.

o.rationale: Remaining field services still require accountable operators, trained crews, equipment, permits, and safety compliance. Mine owners may insource work or deploy autonomous equipment, but software alone cannot deliver most physical services.
o.change_evidence: Mine sourcing plans and operating data showing insourcing, autonomous-equipment adoption, or elimination of specific contractor work packages.
caveats: Demand lost from lower coal output is captured in d5 rather than repeated here.

narrative.executive_view: Coal-support contracting is labor-heavy and operator-required, but the addressable firm universe is small and underlying production demand is structurally declining. AI can improve coordination and compliance work without replacing most field delivery.
narrative.ai_impact: Practical uses include bid and scope analysis, dispatch, shift reporting, safety-document drafting, maintenance prediction, parts planning, sensor anomaly triage, production reconciliation, and regulatory record search. Drilling, blasting, excavation, equipment operation, underground work, repair, and safety accountability remain physical.
narrative.value_capture: Specialized equipment, basin familiarity, safety records, and urgent availability can protect margin. Large mine customers, competitive rebids, shrinking local demand, and transparent unit rates can pass savings through quickly.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen estimate indicates only a limited lower-middle-market population. Eligibility must exclude captive affiliates and diversified firms whose coal work is minor, while transferability depends on equipment condition, permits, bonding, labor, and customer concentration.
narrative.demand_durability: Near-term EIA forecasts already show falling U.S. coal output, and long-run retirement pressure is severe. Metallurgical and export coal, outsourcing, mine maintenance, closure, and reclamation soften but do not erase the likely five-year contraction.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Principal risks are mine and customer concentration, policy and commodity volatility, fatalities and safety liabilities, bonding, environmental obligations, equipment capex, labor availability, and liquidation rather than sale during contraction.
narrative.principal_driver: Essential physical field work and compliance preserve operator demand within the surviving coal-production and reclamation base.
narrative.principal_weakness: A small target universe is exposed to structural coal decline and concentrated customers with strong repricing leverage.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Coal Mining
sources.supports: Coal mining employed 41,940 workers; construction and extraction occupations were 55.43%, including construction equipment operators at 12.05%, demonstrating the physical mine-site task mix.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Support Activities for Mining
sources.supports: Broader mining support shows extraction workers at 34.37% and installation, maintenance, and repair workers at 5.77%, grounding the contractor task proxy.

sources.title: STEO Current/Previous Forecast Comparisons: U.S. Coal and Carbon Dioxide Emissions
sources.supports: EIA's April 2026 outlook projects U.S. coal production of 533 million short tons in 2025, 517 in 2026, and 494 in 2027, declines of 3.1% and 4.3%.

sources.title: EIA Releases the Annual Energy Outlook 2026
sources.supports: EIA states coal-plant retirements drive U.S. coal demand lower and projects coal generation could mostly disappear if 2024 power-sector emissions regulations are enforced.
